They argued coaching is highly applied, so observation, supervised reps, and mentorship/apprenticeship are ideal for skill development (cueing, troubleshooting, rapport, session adjustments). Greg suggested that if mentorship is possible, even a short period can speed competency. In real-world gyms, many trainers begin before feeling fully ready and improve through deliberate practice. Their practical advice was to seek shadowing/internship opportunities and use low-client-load periods to learn from experienced coaches.

evals.prompt: What’s the best way to read and interpret research papers, and why can one paper take 5-6 hours?
evals.expected_output: They said 5-6 hours can be normal when aiming for deep, critical understanding, especially in unfamiliar domains. In familiar domains, review time can drop substantially because methods, stats, and prior literature are already in your working set. Their workflow included: skim abstract for orientation, identify actual hypotheses, scrutinize methods/statistics, and then contextualize findings against prior studies. A strong practical recommendation was to form your own interpretation first, then compare with higher-experience analysts to calibrate mistakes. Over time, accumulated error-correction is what makes reading both faster and better.

evals.prompt: I've seen carnivore/keto/paleo people say vegan and plant-heavy diets are full of anti-nutrients. Is that actually true, and if so how worried should people be?
evals.expected_output: Eric said anti-nutrients are real (for example phytates, tannins, lectins, and oxalates), and they can reduce absorption of some nutrients in some meal contexts. But he emphasized that this risk is often overstated because many objectively healthy foods contain anti-nutrients, and regular cooking/processing methods reduce many of their effects. He advised against avoiding plant foods wholesale, since that would throw away a lot of high-quality micronutrient sources. Practical guidance was to eat a varied diet with diverse food sources and preparation methods, and if someone is fully plant-based, aim above bare-minimum micronutrient targets as a buffer. He also noted that population data in vegetarians/vegans does not show catastrophic anti-nutrient-driven deficiency patterns. Bottom line: anti-nutrients exist, but for most people they are not something to lose sleep over.

evals.prompt: If I've been training hypertrophy for months, should I re-sensitize my muscles by taking 1-2 weeks off or by doing a 4-5 week strength-focused block?
evals.expected_output: Greg said the best direct evidence comes from Aguiar/Ogasawara-style periodic detraining studies in untrained lifters, where continuous training and repeated 6-weeks-on/3-weeks-off approaches ended up in roughly similar places over the study window. He emphasized that these data are limited, somewhat equivocal, and not strong enough to justify broad long-term prescriptions for trained lifters. His practical recommendation was to periodize within the hypertrophy-relevant rep range instead of full layoffs: for example, move from roughly 12-15 reps toward ~5-6 reps across months, then cycle back up. That creates a novel stimulus shift without abandoning training. He also pointed out that soreness after a layoff does not prove superior long-term hypertrophy. Net takeaway: don't assume you must fully de-train to keep gaining.

evals.prompt: I started drinking a gallon of water per day and now I'm peeing constantly. How much water do I actually need?
evals.expected_output: Eric cited DRI targets of about 3.7 L/day for men and 2.7 L/day for women, and noted these totals include water from beverages plus food (roughly ~80% beverages, ~20% food on typical diets). He said being slightly over target is usually low-risk for healthy people because excess water is excreted, while being meaningfully under-hydrated can impair performance as dehydration approaches ~2%+ body mass loss. He cautioned against extreme water-chugging challenges because hyponatremia is a real risk in extreme scenarios. For most lifters, a rough target around three-quarters to one gallon can be reasonable, adjusted upward if sweat losses are high. Core recommendation: hydrate across the day rather than trying to backload intake late.

evals.prompt: Is it better to sip water all day versus chugging, and can reducing fluids before bed help me stop waking up to pee?
evals.expected_output: Eric said the key issue is hydration timing, not magical differences in absorption from sipping versus chugging. Chugging large amounts in a short window tends to produce rapid clear-urine losses and can leave you under-hydrated for much of the day. His practical rule was to get hydrated and stay hydrated by starting early and maintaining intake steadily. For nighttime urination, he recommended tapering fluids in the final 2-3 hours before bed if nocturia is disrupting sleep, while still avoiding going to bed dehydrated. He also suggested monitoring urine color as a simple day-to-day hydration heuristic (with caveats, since things like vitamins or beets can alter color). This frames hydration as a balance between daytime status and sleep quality.

evals.prompt: Why are deadlifts considered more fatiguing than squats, and should deadlift volume usually be lower?
evals.expected_output: Greg pushed back on the blanket claim that deadlifts are inherently more fatiguing, noting data (including work from Zourdos' lab context) showing similar fatigue outcomes after squat vs deadlift sessions. He proposed several practical contributors: hand/forearm stress from heavy pulling, greater tolerance in sumo versus conventional for some lifters, and the ability to complete deadlifts with more spinal flexion than squats, which may create deeper low-back fatigue. He also highlighted path dependency: many lifters build years of higher squat exposure and lower deadlift exposure, so deadlift tolerance starts lower and appears more fatiguing by history rather than biology. He compared Olympic pulling variants too, where athletes often tolerate relatively high pulling frequency/volume. Recommendation was not to assume universal deadlift fragility, but to program based on individual recovery patterns and technical style.

evals.prompt: Do gym altitude masks actually work, and do any gains fade if you're not living at altitude?
evals.expected_output: Eric explained true altitude adaptation is driven by lower oxygen partial pressure over long exposures, often best approached as live high, train low in endurance contexts. He said marketed altitude masks do not replicate altitude physiology because they do not change oxygen partial pressure; they mainly make breathing feel harder. He summarized that controlled studies generally do not show meaningful improvements in VO2max, peak power, or broad aerobic/anaerobic performance outcomes from those masks. A recurring effect is higher perceived exertion/discomfort at the same workload. He noted a narrow possible use case for respiratory muscle training (for specific contexts like high-altitude mountaineering), but that's different from claiming generalized performance benefits. For most lifters and general trainees, these masks are unnecessary.

evals.prompt: Bodybuilders use nicotine gum in prep for appetite and energy. What are the benefits and risks?
evals.expected_output: Greg confirmed nicotine can suppress appetite, but warned this effect habituates, so it is better viewed as a short-term tool rather than a months-long fix. He discussed that evidence in nicotine-naive performance studies does not show robust ergogenic effects, especially compared with caffeine, which does reliably improve strength/power outcomes. He suggested caffeine is usually the better pre-workout stimulant when performance is the goal. On safety, he said data are much stronger for harms of tobacco products than isolated nicotine gum/patch use, though nicotine still behaves as a stimulant and can worsen concerns like hypertension in susceptible people. He also mentioned nicotine's shorter half-life versus caffeine, which can matter for late-day focus with less sleep disruption in some users. Practical framing: non-essential stimulant use carries dependence tradeoffs, and nicotine is not a magic contest-prep performance enhancer.

evals.prompt: Is there really an evidence-based link between cortisol and fat storage (especially belly fat), or is that mostly internet myth?
evals.expected_output: Eric said cortisol is a real and important hormone, and in extreme chronic elevations it can strongly influence body composition (for example Cushing's syndrome patterns like central adiposity with relatively thinner extremities). He explained cortisol can be catabolic in many tissues and has context-dependent effects on fat metabolism, so the mechanism is plausible and real. However, he stressed that common fitness-level fluctuations from normal daily stress or hard training are usually overinterpreted. Most acute within-range changes do not require direct intervention. The practical mistake is treating normal cortisol variability like a pathology. So the relationship exists, but cortisol-belly-fat claims are often oversimplified outside clinical extremes.

evals.prompt: What's the current practical takeaway on cortisol and fat gain if people think they're high-cortisol all the time?
evals.expected_output: Eric drew a three-part distinction: normal acute fluctuations (no action needed), severe chronic psychological stress (address the stressor and recovery environment), and true endocrine pathology (medical management). He said if someone lives in persistent high stress, the intervention target is stress management/life stress reduction, not random cortisol-lowering supplements. If cortisol appears chronically outside reference ranges, that is doctor/endocrinologist territory. He also noted cortisol follows diurnal rhythms, so single snapshots can be misleading without context. This gives a practical decision tree: don't panic about ordinary fluctuations, but don't ignore persistent severe symptoms either. In short, solve the cause, not just the label.

evals.prompt: What do you think about minimalist warm-ups (mostly just barbell warm-up sets) instead of long activation/mobility routines?
evals.expected_output: Greg's answer was conditional: minimalist warm-ups are fine if they reliably prepare you for the session. He framed it as a cost-benefit issue, especially for people with limited gym time, where cutting warm-up from ~30 minutes to ~5-10 minutes can materially increase productive training time. But he warned against making minimalism the goal itself; under-warming can reduce readiness and potentially increase risk if needed mobility/positioning work is removed. His practical method was iterative troubleshooting: remove one warm-up element at a time and keep only what clearly helps session quality. That approach preserves performance while trimming wasted time. So the best warm-up is the minimum effective warm-up for your body and your session demands.

evals.prompt: Are abnormal blood markers expected in natural bodybuilders, especially during overreaching blocks or contest prep (RBC/WBC/testosterone/iron, etc.)?
evals.expected_output: Eric said yes—contextual abnormalities are common and often expected in trained lifters and especially in prep. He noted lifters can show elevated creatinine (muscle mass, diet, creatine use), elevated creatine kinase and related muscle-damage enzymes after hard training, and altered endocrine markers during aggressive phases. In overreaching contexts, cortisol:testosterone ratio shifts and inflammatory markers can move, while contest prep often shows low testosterone/thyroid and additional immune-related changes. He referenced physique-athlete literature showing hormonal suppression during prep with substantial recovery over following months post-show. He also noted that prep's low calories, food monotony, and heavy training can create short-term insufficiency patterns in some nutrients. Main takeaway: interpret labs through the lens of training phase, diet, and body composition status rather than isolated reference ranges alone.

evals.prompt: Can you gain muscle while eating around maintenance calories if protein is high and you still have body fat to spare?
evals.expected_output: They said yes, this can happen via body recomposition in the right context. The key qualifiers were training status, current body composition, the quality of training stimulus, and adequate protein intake. People with more fat mass to spare are generally better candidates for gaining lean mass without a clear surplus. They framed this as possible rather than guaranteed, and highly individual. The practical takeaway was that a large caloric surplus is not always required to add muscle, especially early in training or when retraining.

evals.prompt: For new lifters, is there any case for starting less evidence-based with things like bro splits, dreamer bulks, and even some ego lifting because they might build useful physical or psychological adaptations?
evals.expected_output: They argued that evidence-based training does not mean overcomplicated or joyless training. They noted that bro splits can still work well, and that pushing hard in the gym can build confidence and effort tolerance when technique is acceptable. On dreamer bulks, they acknowledged these are often not physiologically optimal, but said aggressive phases can help some lifters stop under-eating and unlock progress. They emphasized that beginners often leave gains on the table by being too afraid of gaining fat. They also pushed back on the idea that beginner gains are only tied to a short timeline, suggesting big progress can occur whenever stimulus and adherence improve. The recommendation was to keep core principles intact while allowing a style that helps consistency and buy-in.

evals.prompt: What are some practical high-volume, low-calorie meals or food combinations to stay full while cutting and trying to get very lean?
evals.expected_output: They gave a long list of concrete options centered on lean protein plus very high-volume vegetables. Examples included burrito bowls built on cauliflower rice, shredded chicken with yogurt-based sauces, egg-white omelets with vegetables, lean beef and broccoli, and Greek-yogurt desserts with low-calorie flavor add-ins. They also recommended bulking up ground-beef meals with shredded cabbage, including roughly a 1:1 beef-to-cabbage ratio to substantially increase food volume. Another tip was low-calorie slaw variants using yogurt, mustard, and non-caloric sweetener to keep calories down while improving satiety. The overall strategy was to maximize fiber/water volume and protein density, not just rely on tiny portions of calorie-dense foods. The guidance was very meal-prep oriented and meant to improve adherence in deeper cuts.

evals.prompt: Is trying to train for strength and hypertrophy at the same time basically useless for recreational intermediate lifters? Does it make sense to mix goals by body part or day?
evals.expected_output: Greg said that framing is wrong: concurrent strength and hypertrophy work is usually effective for most non-elite lifters. He suggested two practical templates: (1) mostly hypertrophy work while sprinkling in occasional heavy singles around ~90-92% to keep high-load skill sharp, or (2) block-style programming with about 75% of blocks focused on hypertrophy and every fourth block more strength-specific. He noted that near-max, low-volume peaking work can temporarily limit hypertrophy if done constantly, but you only need short doses of it (e.g., a few weeks every 4-6 months for many lifters). For long-term strength, he emphasized that adding muscle is foundational, so hypertrophy training should be a major part of the year. Bottom line: build size most of the time, keep some heavy practice in, and both outcomes can improve together.

evals.prompt: Do triceps become the prime mover near max bench loads, and does that mean I should prioritize triceps accessories over pec-focused work?
evals.expected_output: Greg argued the claim is overstated and mostly a measurement artifact from looking at the long head of the triceps. He explained that long-head triceps is biarticular and can be de-emphasized at lower loads because one of its joint actions conflicts with the bench movement, then appears to ramp up disproportionately at max loads. He contrasted this with evidence showing benching tends to grow lateral triceps more than long-head triceps, citing a study where long-head growth was minimal. He also referenced Duffy's dissertation data suggesting pecs, delts, and lateral triceps all increase activation similarly as load rises. So he rejected the idea that triceps suddenly become the prime mover at max loads. Practical takeaway: don’t abandon pec work based on that interpretation; train bench-relevant musculature broadly.

evals.prompt: Are some muscles resistant to hypertrophy? Could you actually build a bigger tongue with enough loading, and are calves somehow different?
evals.expected_output: Greg’s answer was that tongues are normal skeletal muscle and likely not uniquely resistant to growth. He discussed sleep-apnea literature showing tongue size varies and can increase with fat deposition, which supports that tongues are modifiable tissue rather than fixed-size structures. He speculated that if a muscle responds to anabolic stimuli (including steroid exposure), it is plausible it would also respond to mechanical loading, though he noted direct progressive-overload tongue studies are lacking. He repeatedly framed parts of this as informed speculation, not clinical advice. His practical conclusion was that calves are not made of some fundamentally different non-growable tissue. In short: no strong evidence for a special hypertrophy-resistant class here, just a weird example with limited direct training data.

evals.prompt: For older lifters, are glucosamine and chondroitin useful for preventing or repairing joint wear and tear?
evals.expected_output: Greg said older evidence (2010 meta-analysis) was largely negative, but newer evidence (2018 meta-analysis covering 26 articles/30 trials) suggested small benefits in osteoarthritis populations. Specifically, he summarized that glucosamine showed a small effect on stiffness, while chondroitin showed small effects on pain and physical function. He emphasized these are modest effects, not dramatic improvements, and combination evidence is less clear. He also highlighted that the better evidence is mostly in osteoarthritis patients, so generalizing to everyone without OA is shaky. Safety looked generally acceptable with mostly mild GI side effects at higher doses. Practical advice: manage expectations and coordinate with a clinician/PT if pain and function are the main concern.

evals.prompt: What signs suggest I need extra conditioning to keep progressing in strength or hypertrophy? What type of conditioning should I do?
evals.expected_output: Greg said the key sign is when required training volume for progress outpaces your ability to complete sessions or recover from them. Instead of defaulting to separate cardio blocks, he now prefers specific conditioning by stepping down the intensity ladder within your lifting. For strength-focused lifters struggling with triples, he suggested spending time in higher-rep sets (roughly 5-8) to build work capacity, then returning to lower reps. For hypertrophy work (e.g., 8-12), he suggested phases with higher reps (12-15 or even 20-25) to raise metabolic tolerance and session capacity. He still likes low-intensity cardio like walking for general health, but not as the first lever for this specific problem. Core recommendation: keep the movement patterns similar and condition yourself via higher-rep versions of your main training.

evals.prompt: How much does food quality really matter for strength and hypertrophy if calories and protein are already decent? Am I losing meaningful gains by eating more fun foods?
evals.expected_output: Their answer was that it depends, with big modifiers: overall health status, age/body composition, and whether your diet creates real nutrient deficiencies. Greg said if someone is young, generally healthy, active, meeting calories/protein, and not deficient, the direct strength/hypertrophy penalty from imperfect food quality is probably small. He thought improving diet quality in that case may matter more for feeling better and long-term health than for huge short-term gains differences. They both emphasized that many concerns are about missing beneficial foods/nutrients rather than the mere presence of enjoyable calorie-dense foods. They also noted context where less nutrient-dense additions can be useful, like helping athletes meet high energy demands. Practical takeaway: secure essentials (energy, protein, micronutrient adequacy), then treat food-quality upgrades as an important but context-dependent performance and health lever.

evals.prompt: How much does protein feeding increase muscle protein synthesis compared with resistance training itself? Are we over-focusing on protein source and timing details?
evals.expected_output: They said lifting is the dominant driver of muscle protein synthesis, while protein strategy is more of a permissive support factor. Eric used examples from androgen-deprivation and immobilization research to show how large the training effect can be relative to nutrition tweaks. During layoffs, they noted that even very reduced training volume (roughly 10-15% of normal, i.e., an 85-90% cut) can still help preserve muscle and strength. By contrast, protein-only interventions during immobilization are usually underwhelming, with mixed small benefits in some higher-dose amino acid/leucine studies. Their practical hierarchy was to keep some safe muscle loading whenever possible, then optimize protein amount/distribution/timing on top of that.

evals.prompt: Why don’t exercise studies routinely track and report participant sleep? Even imperfect app data seems better than nothing.
evals.expected_output: They argued the main issue is evidence and feasibility, not ignorance. Eric said there is surprisingly limited long-duration resistance-training evidence directly showing that chronic short sleep changes hypertrophy/strength outcomes, so sleep is not always treated as a must-control covariate yet. He also emphasized measurement quality: self-report and many consumer trackers are noisy versus gold-standard sleep methods. With small sample sizes common in exercise science, adding many weak covariates can worsen analyses rather than improve them. So their view was that better longitudinal data and better measurement pipelines are needed before sleep can be consistently modeled in these trials.

evals.prompt: How should I respond when a doctor tells me to stop squatting because it’s bad for my knees?
evals.expected_output: They recommended starting from empathy for the clinician’s context: general practitioners usually prioritize reducing symptoms and preserving activities of daily living, not maximizing squat performance. In that framework, conservative advice to stop a painful non-essential activity can be expected. But they also said lifters should seek second opinions from sports medicine professionals or PTs who understand training goals. They framed this as a cost-benefit decision where your risk tolerance and priorities matter. The practical message was: don’t automatically dismiss medical advice, but get context-specific guidance from providers experienced with athletes.

evals.prompt: After hernia surgery with temporary lifting restrictions, how can I minimize muscle loss and when should I return to training?
evals.expected_output: They said nutrition alone is usually weak for preventing detraining losses compared with actual loading. Eric noted that studies using very high leucine intakes (about 15-19 g/day extra) sometimes show partial benefit, while lower doses (around 10 g/day or less extra) are often not very effective. Even then, he emphasized that safe tissue loading is still the key if medically allowed. For return-to-training timing, they refused to give a universal timeline and said to work closely with your surgeon/clinical team, even if that means asking very specific follow-up questions. They also stressed that regaining lost strength is typically faster than building it the first time, so being conservative short-term is usually the better long-term play.

evals.prompt: If my sleep schedule is sporadic, what should I do for body composition, recovery, and training? Are naps useful, and should I ever train sleep deprived?
evals.expected_output: They said poor sleep often worsens body composition via appetite/self-control effects, so controlling food environment becomes extra important when sleep deprived. For training, they advised keeping resistance training in place but biasing toward safer options (machines, dumbbells, controlled bodybuilding-style work) when motor control is compromised. They cautioned against high-risk sessions like max free-weight attempts or hard heat-exposed conditioning when severely sleep deprived. On naps, they said short naps around 20-30 minutes are often useful, while some people tolerate longer ~90-minute naps if nighttime sleep is not disrupted. Overall guidance: keep training, prioritize safety, and manage sleep-driven behavior drift rather than abandoning training.

evals.prompt: How do we define training hard enough for typical lifters trying to get stronger or improve physique?
evals.expected_output: They framed this around progressive overload and recoverability, not constant all-out effort. Eric said many productive sets can live around 0-4 reps in reserve (RIR), often starting farther from failure and moving closer across a block. They also discussed volume as a lever: if progress stalls and deloads are almost never needed, you may be under-dosing training stress. Greg added practical heuristics that repeatedly needing deloads every ~4-8 weeks can indicate productive difficulty, whereas crashing every 1-2 weeks suggests too much. Their short version was 'harder than last time,' while still preserving safety and long-term consistency.

evals.prompt: If I hit my calorie target but I’m under my protein target, should I go over calories to hit protein? What if this happens often?
evals.expected_output: They agreed the first fix is upstream: if this is frequent, adjust calorie targets, protein targets, meal planning, or food environment so targets are realistically compatible. Eric gave a common baseline of about 1.6 g/kg/day protein as a practical middle target (with higher needs in some deficits) and suggested lowering unrealistic protein goals when needed. For rare misses, both said the difference is usually negligible either way. In deficits, Eric leaned toward prioritizing protein and accepting a small calorie overshoot (for example +200 kcal once in a while), while Greg often prioritizes preserving the planned deficit depending on phase priority. For recomp/lean-bulk contexts they suggested splitting the difference (e.g., hit ~85-90% of protein target without overshooting calories as much).

evals.prompt: How should bodybuilders periodize compound lifts to improve hypertrophy? Do they need sophisticated periodization blocks?
evals.expected_output: They said bodybuilding generally does not require elaborate multi-capacity periodization because the main objective is hypertrophy almost year-round. Instead, they favored simple block management: control RIR, accumulate enough volume, deload when fatigue warrants, and rotate emphasis as weak points change. Eric said some variation is still useful for enjoyment, staleness management, and practical recovery. He also highlighted that prep phases can justify shifts toward somewhat higher intensity/lower volume as cardio increases and glycogen/energy availability drops. Bottom line: use practical autoregulated programming adjustments rather than forcing overly complex periodization models.

evals.prompt: If ethics and money were no concern, what study would you most want to run?
evals.expected_output: Greg's provocative example was a definitive human hyperplasia study, since counting muscle fibers directly is ethically infeasible in normal research. His realistic preference was a massive mechanistic and responder-variability project with very large samples, rich omics data, and multiple training programs to map why individuals respond differently. Eric added several high-priority practical trials: a properly powered creatine-caffeine interaction trial, well-designed nitrate plus citrulline hypertrophy studies with adequate doses, and better protein-dose scaling studies across body compositions and energy states. They also called out a major gap on how to train during caloric deficits (maintain volume vs maintain intensity), noting there is strong opinion but little direct data.

evals.prompt: How does very low body fat affect strength and hypertrophy progress, and how can someone know if they’re too lean given poor body-fat measurement tools?
evals.expected_output: They said very low body fat generally makes both performance and hypertrophy progress much harder due to reduced energy availability and impaired recovery. Eric described practical signs during contest-level leanness: worse training quality, slower recovery, colder body temperature, higher hunger, and broad 'system slowdown' effects. He also noted that in their p-ratio review, substantial lean-mass gains were uncommon below roughly 8% body fat in men. For monitoring, they advised against over-relying on imperfect body-fat devices and instead emphasized symptom-based and performance-based feedback. In other words, individual functional signs of low energy availability are more actionable than chasing one exact body-fat number.
evals.topic: very low body fat and training outcomes

evals.prompt: What fitness beliefs are you most against, relative to common wisdom?
evals.expected_output: Greg pushed back on the premise that one unified 'common wisdom' exists across all fitness subcultures. His main concrete example was stretching: he argued static stretching is often unfairly maligned in lifting circles, despite strong evidence that chronic stretching can improve flexibility. He acknowledged acute pre-performance reductions can occur with very long/intense stretching immediately before maximal tests, but said those effects often dissipate after normal warm-up. He also used training frequency as an example of shifting consensus over time, showing how 'common wisdom' can reverse within a decade. So their broader answer was that context and subcommunity matter more than any single universal orthodoxy.

evals.prompt: If I train in the morning (often fasted) and perform a bit worse, how much does that matter for non-competitive goals like health or hypertrophy?
evals.expected_output: They said the impact depends on goal hierarchy: for health-focused training, there is lots of wiggle room, while performance-specific goals require tighter optimization. For hypertrophy, they still view session performance as meaningful because mechanical tension and productive reps matter, even if perfection is unnecessary. On fasted training specifically, they highlighted mixed evidence and said hunger itself may hurt performance even when meal energy content differences are less clear. Their conservative recommendation was a small pre-workout feeding if tolerated (for example at least ~20 g carbohydrate, optionally with protein, and usually low fat). If food before training causes GI distress, they advised adapting timing/form (including liquid options) rather than forcing a strategy that worsens the session.

evals.prompt: From a statistics perspective, what actually supports causal claims? Which analyses imply causation?
evals.expected_output: They said causation comes primarily from study design, not from picking a 'causal' statistical test. Eric emphasized that observational correlations alone usually cannot establish cause, while well-controlled randomized trials can support causal inference because of randomization and control, not because of t-tests/ANOVA themselves. He also warned that advanced modeling (like SEM) does not magically convert weak observational data into definitive causal proof. They referenced Bradford Hill criteria as one framework for weighing causal evidence in epidemiology. Greg added that in human in-vivo work, causal claims are usually partial/probabilistic causes rather than strict one-to-one deterministic causation.

evals.prompt: Is cereal a soup?
evals.expected_output: Both hosts said yes, with surprising conviction. Their argument was that soup does not need to be hot and can include sweet forms, including cold dessert soups in multiple cuisines. They treated cereal-plus-milk as fitting the broad structure of solids in a liquid base. They also joked that broad soup taxonomy supports other contentious food classifications. Net conclusion: cereal qualifies as soup in their framework.

evals.prompt: I saw protein overfeeding studies where people ate around 800 extra calories but didn’t gain fat. Can amino acids actually be converted and stored as body fat, or are those calories somehow not usable?
evals.expected_output: Eric said the mechanistic answer is yes: excess protein calories can be oxidized for energy and can ultimately contribute to fat storage. He emphasized that thermic effect differs by macro (protein highest, fat lowest), so storage efficiency differs, but that does not make protein calories 'free.' He was skeptical of interpreting the Antonio-style free-living overfeeding papers literally because they rely on self-reported intake. He cited overfeeding work (including Han et al.) showing people often fail to truly hit prescribed protein surpluses, and a controlled metabolic-ward trial by Bray et al. where intake was supervised and overfeeding behaved as expected. In that ward study, protein ranged roughly from ~0.7-0.8 g/kg to ~3 g/kg and participants still gained fat mass under sustained caloric surplus. Practical takeaway: high protein can make overfeeding harder because of satiety, but it does not bypass energy balance.

evals.prompt: Why do I sometimes ache more during a deload week than during hard training weeks?
evals.expected_output: Eric said there is not solid direct human evidence here, so explanations are tentative rather than definitive. One possibility is that hard training acutely elevates sympathetic output/catecholamines (adrenaline-related) that can transiently blunt pain perception. Another is that general movement itself has an analgesic effect, so when total activity drops during deloads, discomfort can feel more noticeable. A third possibility is attentional: during hard training you are focused on performance, while during deloads you may notice background aches more. His bottom line was that several mechanisms are plausible, none are proven, and high-confidence single-cause explanations are overstated.

evals.prompt: What do you think about ashwagandha for performance and strength? A meta-analysis says it helps.
evals.expected_output: Greg said the meta-analysis itself looked competently done, but he does not think the underlying evidence base is consistent enough to strongly recommend ashwagandha as an ergogenic aid. He highlighted very large, hard-to-generalize effect sizes in some studies (for example, an ~8-week trial reporting about +46 kg bench gain in the ashwagandha group, with very large placebo gains too), which suggests high noise and limited external validity rather than a reliable expected effect. He also noted weak mechanistic coherence across studies. His practical recommendation was cautious: if someone is very interested, uses a reputable product, and stays within labeled dosing, trying it is reasonable, but expectations should be modest. He does not view current evidence as strong enough for confident performance claims.

evals.prompt: How have your lifts changed during your cut, and what helped you maintain strength while losing weight?
evals.expected_output: Greg said he did get weaker over the cut, but he attributed much of that to disrupted training rather than fat loss itself. He started cutting around February and, in April/May, injured his wrist/elbow, which limited gripping, squatting setups, and pressing and led to a few months of minimal training. He said this break helped him psychologically accept temporary strength loss so the diet could continue. Once he resumed consistent lifting, strength rebounded quickly (for example, returning to a hard 315 single, then a relatively comfortable 315 triple on low incline within a few sessions). His framing was that consistency and acceptance mattered more than chasing perfect in-cut peak numbers, and that retraining restored performance rapidly.

evals.prompt: For body composition, is HIIT better than moderate steady-state cardio? And what interval setups do you recommend?
evals.expected_output: Eric summarized a meta-analysis (Steele et al.) finding no meaningful body-composition advantage of interval vs steady-state cardio: both produced small average changes (about -0.25 kg fat and +0.1 kg fat-free mass). He noted cardio is great for health and can help, but as a standalone fat-loss tool it is usually modest, so pairing with diet is more effective. For intervals, he gave concrete options: 30 s all-out (RPE 10) + 90 s easy recovery for 10-15 rounds; or 1 min hard / 1 min easy around RPE 9-10; or 2 min hard / 2 min easy at slightly lower intensity (about RPE 8-9.5) for fewer rounds. He also said interference with lifting is usually small if total stress is managed and sessions are programmed sensibly. Practical takeaway: choose the modality/protocol you can perform safely and adhere to.
evals.topic: HIIT vs steady-state for fat loss

evals.prompt: How much benefit do you get from individualized training or coaching versus a general program, and when does personalization matter most?
evals.expected_output: Greg said direct resistance-training evidence is limited, but conceptually supportive findings exist. He cited a force-velocity-profile jump study where individualized programming outperformed a generalized approach for jump outcomes, while acknowledging this is proof-of-concept rather than direct maximal-strength evidence. He also pointed out supervision research showing that even on the same program, more direct coaching tends to improve strength outcomes. From within-subject unilateral studies, he suggested individual differences appear most meaningful for volume/frequency tolerance; in one frequency-volume design (e.g., 2x vs 3x vs 5x), higher frequency won on average but some individuals did better at lower frequencies. His applied recommendation was to individualize volume and frequency first, since other variables likely matter less for most people.
